summary:
The Plymouth Colony (1620–1691 AD) was the second successful English settlement in North America, established by a group of Separatist Puritans (the 'Pilgrims') who crossed the Atlantic aboard the Mayflower in 1620 fleeing religious persecution, and whose founding mythology — particularly the 'First Thanksgiving' — became central to American national identity despite the colony's modest scale and eventual absorption into Massachusetts Bay Colony. The 102 passengers aboard the Mayflower included both Separatist religious refugees who had been exiled to the Netherlands and 'Strangers' (non-Separatists) hired by the Virginia Company; landing at Plymouth (in present-day Massachusetts) rather than their intended Virginia destination, they had no legal charter authorizing their settlement.

To establish a legal basis for self-governance, the adult male passengers signed the Mayflower Compact (November 11, 1620) before landing — a foundational document of American democracy in which the signers agreed to create a 'civil body politic' and govern themselves by majority rule, drawing on Protestant covenant theology as its intellectual foundation. The colony's first winter was devastating: about half the settlers died of cold, disease, and malnutrition. The Wampanoag Native American Squanto (Tisquantum), who spoke English from earlier contact with European traders, helped the survivors learn local agricultural techniques and brokered a peace treaty with the Wampanoag sachem Massasoit that lasted 50 years. The 'First Thanksgiving' feast (autumn 1621), a three-day harvest celebration attended by both colonists and Wampanoag, was a real event, though its transformation into a national founding myth involved centuries of selective memory that omitted the subsequent catastrophic devastation of New England's indigenous population.
